title: How to use 'collate_fn' with dataloaders?
error_text: TypeError: vars() argument must have dict attribute
answer:
Root Cause: Basically, the `collate_fn` receives a list of tuples if your `__getitem__` function from a Dataset subclass returns a tuple, or just a normal list if your Dataset subclass returns only one element.
Fix: Try to see it as a glue that you specify the way examples stick together in a batch. If you don’t use it, PyTorch only put `batch_size` examples together as you would using torch.stack (not exactly it, but it is simple like that).
Code:
def collate_fn(data):
    """
       data: is a list of tuples with (example, label, length)
             where 'example' is a tensor of arbitrary shape
             and label/length are scalars
    """
    _, labels, lengths = zip(*data)
    max_len = max(lengths)

title: pytorch: "multi-target not supported" error message

answer:
Root Cause: For `` the target has to be a single number from the interval [0, #classes] instead of a one-hot encoded target vector.
Fix: Replace your one-hot-encoded targets: [1, 0] --> 0 [0, 1] --> 1

nn.CrossEntropyLoss

title: Pytorch ValueError: optimizer got an empty parameter list
error_text: ValueError: optimizer got an empty parameter list
answer:
Root Cause: Your `NetActor` does not directly store any `nn.Parameter`. Moreover, all other layers it eventually uses in `forward` are stored as a simple list in `self.nn_layers`.
Fix: If you want `self.actor_nn.parameters()` to know that the items stored in the list `self.nn_layers` may contain trainable parameters, you should work with containers.
Code:
self.nn_layers = nn.ModuleList()

title: PyTorch - How to deactivate dropout in evaluation mode
error_text: TypeError: forward() missing 1 required positional argument: 'p'
answer:
Root Cause: You have to define your `nn.Dropout` layer in your `__init__` and assign it to your model to be responsive for calling `eval()`.
Fix: NOTE: If you want to continue training afterwards you need to call `train()` on your model to leave evaluation mode. You can also find a small working example for dropout with `eval()` for evaluation mode here: nn.Dropout vs.
Code:
class mylstm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self,input_dim, output_dim, hidden_dim,linear_dim,p):
        super(mylstm, self).__init__()
        self.hidden_dim=hidden_dim
        self.lstm=nn.LSTMCell(input_dim,self.hidden_dim)
        self.linear1=nn.Linear(hidden_dim,linear_dim)

title: How can I generate and display a grid of images in PyTorch with plt.imshow and torchvision.utils.make_grid?
error_text: TypeError: Invalid dimensions for image data
answer:
Root Cause: There's a small mistake in your code. `torchvision.utils.make_grid()` returns a tensor which contains the grid of images.
Fix: But the channel dimension has to be moved to the end since that's what matplotlib recognizes. Below is the code that works fine:  which shows the output as:
Code:
In [107]: import torchvision
# sample input (10 RGB images containing just Gaussian Noise)
In [108]: batch_tensor = torch.randn(*(10, 3, 256, 256))   # (N, C, H, W)
# make grid (2 rows and 5 columns) to display our 10 images
In [109]: grid_img = torchvision.utils.make_grid(batch_tensor, nrow=5)

title: Autograd.grad() for Tensor in pytorch
error_text: RunTimeerror: grad can be implicitly created only for scalar outputs”`.
answer:
Root Cause: Let's start from simple working example with plain loss function and regular backward. We will build short computational graph and do some grad computations on it.
Fix: Code:  Output: `Loss: 42.25 dloss/dx: (tensor([[-19.5000, -19.5000], [-19.5000, -19.5000]]),) ` Ok, this works! Now let's try to reproduce error "grad can be implicitly created only for scalar outputs".
Code:
import torch
from torch.autograd import grad
import torch.nn as nn
# Create some dummy data.
x = torch.ones(2, 2, requires_grad=True)
gt = torch.ones_like(x) * 16 - 0.5  # "ground-truths"
# We will use MSELoss as an example.
loss_fn = nn.MSELoss()

title: img should be PIL Image. Got
error_text: TypeError: img should be PIL Image. Got `
answer:
Root Cause: `transforms.RandomHorizontalFlip()` works on `PIL.Images`, not `torch.Tensor`. In your code above, you are applying `transforms.ToTensor()` prior to `transforms.RandomHorizontalFlip()`, which results in tensor.
Fix: But, as per the official pytorch documentation here, transforms.RandomHorizontalFlip() horizontally flip the given PIL Image randomly with a given probability. So, just change the order of your transformation in above code, like below:
Code:
train_transforms = transforms.Compose([transforms.Resize(255),
                                       transforms.CenterCrop(224),
                                       transforms.RandomHorizontalFlip(),
                                       transforms.ToTensor(),

title: pytorch RuntimeError: Expected object of scalar type Double but got scalar type Float
error_text: RuntimeError: Expected object of scalar type Double but got scalar type Float for argument #2 'mat2' in call to _th_mm
answer:
Root Cause: In short: your data has type double but your model has type float, this is not allowed in pytorch because only data with the same dtype can be fed into the model.
Fix: I will first explain why this error happens and then suggest some solutions(but I think you will not need my solution once you understand the principle.) PyTorch has a couple of dtypes https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html.
Code:
torch.float32

title: The size of tensor a (707) must match the size of tensor b (512) at non-singleton dimension 1
answer:
Root Cause: This is because, BERT uses word-piece tokenization. So, when some of the words are not in the vocabulary, it splits the words to it's word pieces.
Fix: For example: if the word `` is not in the vocabulary, it can split down to `play, ##ing`. This increases the amount of tokens in a given sentence after tokenization.
Code:
playing

title: Pytorch: RuntimeError: reduce failed to synchronize: cudaErrorAssert: device-side assert triggered
error_text: RuntimeError: reduce failed to synchronize: cudaErrorAssert: device-side assert triggered
answer:
Root Cause: This kind of error generally occurs when using `` or `CrossEntropyLoss`, and when your dataset has negative labels (or labels greater than the number of classes).
Fix: The solution is to clean the dataset and ensure that `t >= 0 && t Also, ensure that your network output is in the range 0 to 1 in case you use `` or `BCELoss` (then you require `softmax` or `sigmoid` activation respectively).

NLLLoss

answer:
Root Cause: Cause: You trained a model derived from `resnet18` in this way:  That is, you changed the last `nn.Linear` layer to output 4 dim prediction instead of the default 1000.
Fix: Fix: (1) Apply the same change before loading the `checkpoint`: `model = resnet18(pretrained=True) num_ftrs = model_ft.fc.in_features model_ft.fc = nn.Linear(num_ftrs, 4) # make the change model.load_state_dict(checkpoint) # load ` (2).
Code:
model_ft = models.resnet18(pretrained=True)
num_ftrs = model_ft.fc.in_features
model_ft.fc = nn.Linear(num_ftrs, 4)

title: Problem with missing and unexpected keys while loading my model in Pytorch
error_text: RuntimeError: Error(s) in loading state_dict for Network:
answer:
Root Cause: So your `Network` is essentially the `classifier` part of `AlexNet` and you're looking to load pretrained `AlexNet` weights into it.
Fix: You want to Keep only the tensors with name starting with "classifier." Remove the "classifier." part of keys so try
Code:
model = Network()
loaded_dict = checkpoint['model_state_dict']
prefix = 'classifier.'
n_clip = len(prefix)
adapted_dict = {k[n_clip:]: v for k, v in loaded_dict.items()
                if k.startswith(prefix)}
model.load_state_dict(adapted_dict)

title: Understanding input shape to PyTorch conv1D?
error_text: RuntimeError: Given groups=1, weight of size 100 768 2, expected input `[4, 512, 768]` to have 768 channels, but got 512 channels instead
answer:
Root Cause: In pytorch your input shape of [6, 512, 768] should actually be [6, 768, 512] where the feature length is represented by the channel dimension and sequence length is the length dimension.
Fix: Given an `input` of shape [6, 512, 768] you can convert it to the correct shape with `Tensor.transpose`.  The `.contiguous()` ensures the memory of the tensor is stored contiguously which helps avoid potential issues during processing.
Code:
input = input.transpose(1, 2).contiguous()

title: RuntimeError: Given groups=1, weight of size [32, 3, 16, 16, 16], expected input[100, 16, 16, 16, 3] to have 3 channels, but got 16 channels instead
error_text: RuntimeError: Given groups=1, weight of size [32, 3, 16, 16, 16], expected input[100, 16, 16, 16, 3] to have 3 channels, but got 16 channels instead
answer:
Root Cause: `nn.Conv3d` expects the input to have size [batch_size, channels, depth, height, width]. The first convolution expects 3 channels, but with your input having size [100, 16, 16, 16, 3], that would be 16 channels.
Fix: Assuming that your data is given as [batch_size, depth, height, width, channels], you need to swap the dimensions around, which can be done with `torch.Tensor.permute`:
Code:
# From: [batch_size, depth, height, width, channels]
# To: [batch_size, channels, depth, height, width]
input = input.permute(0, 4, 1, 2, 3)

title: Pytorch inputs for nn.CrossEntropyLoss()
error_text: RuntimeError: Dimension out of range (expected to be in range of [-1, 0], but got 1)`
answer:
Root Cause: For the most part, the PyTorch documentation does an amazing job to explain the different functions; they usually do include expected input dimensions, as well as some simple examples.
Fix: To walk through your specific example, let us start by looking at the expected input dimension: Input: (N,C) where C = number of classes. [...] To add to this, N generally refers to the batch size (number of samples).
Code:
outputs.shape
>>> torch.Size([2])

title: RuntimeError: The size of tensor a (133) must match the size of tensor b (10) at non-singleton dimension 1
error_text: RuntimeError: The size of tensor a (133) must match the size of tensor b (10) at non-singleton dimension 1.
answer:
Root Cause: Well, the error is because the `` and `nn.CrossEntropyLoss()` expect different `input`/`target` combinations. You cannot simply change the criterion function without changing the inputs and targets appropriately.
Fix: From the docs: `nn.CrossEntropyLoss`: Input: (N, C) where C = number of classes, or (N, C, d_1, d_2, ..., d_K) with K >= 1 in the case of K-dimensional loss.
Code:
nn.MSELoss()

title: No module named ‘torchvision.models.utils‘
error_text: No module named 'torchvision.models.utils'
answer:
Root Cause: After consulting torchvision's code repository, there is a solution: Note that this syntax is only for higher versions of PyTorch. The original code from .utils import load_state_dict_from_url is not applicable.
Fix: you cannot import load_state_dict_from_url from .utils. change .utils to torch.hub can fix the problem.  This worked for me.

from torch.hub import load_state_dict_from_url

title: RuntimeError: one of the variables needed for gradient computation has been modified by an inplace operation?

Hint: the backtrace further above shows the operation that failed to compute its gradient. The variable in question was changed in there or anywhere later. Good luck!

Root Cause: This happens because the opt_D.step() modifies the parameters of your discriminator inplace. But these parameters are required to compute the gradient for the generator.
Fix: You can fix this by changing your code to:  You can find more about this issue here https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/39141

    artist_paintings = artist_works()  # real painting from artist

    prob_artist1 = D(G_paintings)  # G tries to fool D

answer:
Root Cause: It could be that your `` and `col` tensors are not aligned (i.e. one of them is transposed so it is (1,1000000) instead of (1000000,1).
Fix: Then when you do `( * col)` the shapes are broadcast together and it makes a tensor that is (1000000,1000000) which is probably where you are getting the extreme memory usage (as the resulting tensor is 1000000 times bigger than your.

title: How are the pytorch dimensions for linear layers calculated?
error_text: RuntimeError: size mismatch, m1: [1 x 936144], m2: [400 x 3] at /pytorch/aten/src/TH/generic/THTensorMath.cpp:940
answer:
Root Cause: The key step is between the last convolution and the first `Linear` block. `Conv2d` outputs a tensor of shape `[batch_size, n_features_conv, height, width]` whereas `Linear` expects `[batch_size, n_features_lin]`.
Fix: To make the two align you need to "stack" the 3 dimensions `[n_features_conv, height, width]` into one `[n_features_lin]`. As follows, it must be that `n_features_lin == n_features_conv * height * width`.
Code:
x = x.view(-1, self.num_flat_features(x))

title: CUDA out of memory error, cannot reduce batch size
error_text: CUDA out of memory. Tried to allocate 3.63 GiB (GPU 0;
answer:
Root Cause: As long as a single sample can fit into GPU memory, you do not have to reduce the effective batch size: you can do gradient accumulation.
Fix: This is nicely explained in this video. Effectively, your training code would look something like this.
Code:
train_data = DataLoader(train_set, batch_size=small_batch, ...)
opt.zero_grad()  # this signifies the start of a large_batch
for i, (x, y) in train_data:
  pred = model(x)
  loss = criterion(pred, y)
  loss.backward()  # gradeints computed for small_batch

title: Can't install GPU-enabled Pytorch in Conda environment from environment.yml
error_text: AssertionError: Torch not compiled with CUDA enabled
answer:
Root Cause: Just a few minutes after posting this question, I was able to figure out the solution. It turns out that it has to do with prioritizing Conda channels.
Fix: The solution (which isn't well-documented by Anaconda) is to specify the correct channel for `cudatoolkit` and `pytorch` in `environment.yml`:
Code:
name: foo
channels:
  - conda-forge
  - nvidia
  - pytorch
dependencies:
  - nvidia::cudatoolkit=11.1
  - python=3.8

Fix: Create conda env for example: `pytorch_p37` with `python 3.7`:  2.

user@pc:~$ conda create -n pytorch_p37 python=3.7

title: How to convert RGB images to grayscale in PyTorch dataloader?
error_text: TypeError: img should be PIL Image. Got
answer:
Root Cause: When using `ImageFolder` class and with no custom loader, pytorch uses PIL to load image and converts it to RGB.
Fix: It will convert the 3 channel RGB image into 1 channel grayscale.
Code:
def pil_loader(path):
    with open(path, 'rb') as f:
        img = Image.open(f)
        return img.convert('RGB')

title: mat1 and mat2 must have the same dtype
error_text: RuntimeError: mat1 and mat2 must have the same dtype
answer:
Root Cause: The reason for this is because the parameters dtype of `` doesn't match your input's dtype; the default dtype for `` is `torch.float32` which is in your case different from your input data - `float64`.
Fix: In short, add `self.double()` at the end of your constructor and things should run.
Code:
nn.Linear

title: PyTorch running under WSL2 getting "Killed" for Out of memory even though I have a lot of memory left?
error_text: killed
answer:
Root Cause: According to this blog post, WSL2 is automatically configured to use 50% of the physical RAM of the machine.
Fix: After adding this file, shut down your distribution and wait at least 8 seconds before restarting.
Code:
[wsl2]
memory=48GB

T.backward()

Fix: This parameter will load the the embedding and encoding layers of your model, but will randomly initialize the classification head:  In case you want to keep the classification layer of the 59 labels and add 46 labels, you can refer to.

model = BertForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained("finetuned_BERT_epoch_2_full-Copy1.model",
                                                      num_labels=105,

title: Size Mismatch Runtime Error When Trying to Load a PyTorch Model
error_text: RuntimeError: Error(s) in loading state_dict for FasterRCNN:
answer:
Root Cause: As mentioned in previous answers, the error happens because the checkpoint contains weights for a different number of classes.
Fix: You can find here a solution for the error, which involves first removing the layers with mismatched sizes from the state_dict and only then loading it with strict=False. In your case, it would look like this:
Code:
current_model_dict = model.state_dict()
loaded_state_dict = torch.load(path, map_location=torch.device('cpu'))
new_state_dict={k:v if v.size()==current_model_dict[k].size()  else  current_model_dict[k] for k,v in zip(current_model_dict.keys(), loaded_state_dict.values())}

title: Pytorch : Expected all tensors on same device
error_text: RuntimeError: Expected all tensors to be on the same device, but found at least two devices, cpu and cuda:0! (when checking argument for argument mat1 in method wrapper_addmm)
answer:
Root Cause: TL;DR use `` instead of a pythonic one to store the hidden layers in `Net`. All your hidden layers are stored in a simple pythonic list `self.hidden` in `Net`.
Fix: When you move your model to GPU, using `.to(device)`, pytorch has no way to tell that all the elements of this pythonic list should also be moved to the same device.
Code:
nn.ModuleList

title: Understanding why memory allocation occurs during inference, backpropagation, and model update
error_text: CUDA out of memory. Tried to allocate 2.32 GiB (GPU 0; 15.78 GiB total capacity; 11.91 GiB already allocated; 182.75 MiB free; 14.26 GiB reserved in total by PyTorch)
answer:
Root Cause: Inference By default, an inference on your model will allocate memory to store the activations of each layer (activation as in intermediate layer inputs).
Fix: A simple but effective example is a function defined by ``. Here, `df/dx = 2x`, i.e. in order to compute `df/dx` you are required to keep `x` in memory.

f: x -> x²

title: How do I pass a keyword argument to the forward used by a pre-forward hook?
error_text: TypeError: forward() got an unexpected keyword argument 'higgs_bosson'
answer:
Root Cause: Torchscript incompatible (as of `1.2.0`) First of all, your example `torch.nn.Module` has some minor mistakes (probably by an accident).
Fix: You indeed cannot do it without modifying `forward` call, but why would you want to avoid that?
Code:
import torch
class NeoEmbeddings(torch.nn.Embedding):
    def __init__(self, num_embeddings: int, embedding_dim: int, padding_idx=-1):
        super().__init__(num_embeddings, embedding_dim, padding_idx)
        self.register_forward_pre_hook(NeoEmbeddings.neo_genesis)

title: detectron2 installation - No module named 'torch'
error_text: No module named 'torch'
answer:
Root Cause: This is probably due to the isolation mechanism of the pip building process.
Fix: You can disable that isolation by using this command:  This happens a lot for packages that need torch, probably they tend to import it to check for cuda and/or other capabilities and/or to compile some kernels.
Code:
$ pip install --no-build-isolation 'git+https://github.com/facebookresearch/detectron2.git'

title: Can numpy arrays run in GPUs?
error_text: TypeError: can't convert cuda:0 device type tensor to numpy. Use Tensor.cpu() to copy the tensor to host memory first.
answer:
Root Cause: No you cannot generally run numpy functions on GPU arrays. PyTorch reimplements much of the functionality in numpy for PyTorch tensors.
Fix: For example `torch.chunk` works similarly to `np.array_split` so you could do the following:  which splits `X` into multiple tensors without ever moving the `X` off the GPU.
Code:
X = np.array([[1, 3, 2, 3], [2, 3, 5, 6], [1, 2, 3, 4]])
X = torch.DoubleTensor(X).cuda()
X_split = torch.chunk(X, chunks=2, dim=0)

title: With a PyTorch LSTM, can I have a different hidden_size than input_size?
error_text: RuntimeError: The size of tensor a (5) must match the size of tensor b (15) at non-singleton dimension 2
answer:
Root Cause: It should work the error probably came from elsewhere.
Fix: Did you notice it has a size equal to hidden dim ie 5 on last dim ? It looks like you're using it afterwards thinking it has a size of 15 instead
Code:
feature_dim = 15
        hidden_size = 5
        num_layers = 2
        seq_len = 5
        batch_size = 3
        lstm = nn.LSTM(input_size=feature_dim,
                                    hidden_size=hidden_size, num_layers=num_layers)

title: What functions or modules require contiguous input?
error_text: RuntimeError: invalid argument 1: input is not contiguous at .../src/torch/lib/TH/generic/THTensor.c:231
answer:
Root Cause: After additional digging under the hood through source_code, it seems that `view` is the only function that explicitly causes an exception when a non-contiguous input is passed.
Fix: In reality, it seems to be the case that most or all of these functions are: (a.) implemented with support for non-contiguous blocks (see example below), i.e.
Code:
import torch
import numpy
import time
# allocation
start = time.time()
test = torch.rand([10000,1000,100])
torch.cuda.synchronize()
end = time.time()

title: Error while trying to fine-tune the ReformerModelWithLMHead (google/reformer-enwik8) for NER
error_text: ValueError: If training, make sure that config.axial_pos_shape factors: (128, 512) multiply to sequence length. Got prod((128, 512)) != sequence_length: 2248. You might want to consider padding your sequence length to 65536 or changing config.axial_pos_shape.
answer:
Root Cause: First of all, you should note that `google/reformer-enwik8` is not a properly trained language model and that you will probably not get decent results from fine-tuning it.
Fix: This is also the reason why they haven't trained a sub-word tokenizer and operate on character level. You should also note that the `LMHead` is usually used for predicting the next token of a sequence (CLM).
Code:
from transformers import ReformerConfig, ReformerModel
conf = ReformerConfig.from_pretrained('google/reformer-enwik8')
conf.num_hashes = 2 # or maybe even to 1
model = transformers.ReformerModel.from_pretrained("google/reformer-enwik8", config =conf)

title: How do I load up an image and convert it to a proper tensor for PyTorch?
error_text: TypeError: Invalid dimensions for image data
answer:
Root Cause: `` operates on float tensors. It's common and good practice to normalize input images before passing them into the neural network.
Fix: I would add the line `img = img/255` immediately before you convert it to a Torch tensor in `__getitem__`, then it will be converted to a `float` tensor rather than a `byte` tensor and thus will be compatible with the `` method.
Code:
conv2d

title: Using Vicuna + langchain + llama_index for creating a self hosted LLM model

answer:
Root Cause: length is too long, 9999 will consume huge amount of GPU RAM, especially using 13b model. try 7b model.
Fix: And try using something like peft/bitsandbytes to reduce GPU RAM usage. set load_in_8bit=True is a good start.
Code:

title: Cannot import name 'ResNet50_Weights' from 'torchvision.models.resnet'
error_text: ImportError: cannot import name 'ResNet50_Weights' from 'torchvision.models.resnet' (/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.7/lib/python3.7/site-packages/torchvision/models/resnet.py)
answer:
Root Cause: The problem took me some days to solve it. Before running a code containing the Pytorch models, make sure you are connected to a stable network.
Fix: This because for the first time when you are running a Pytorch model such resnet50, alexnet, resnet18 it downloads its' functionalities, so incase of installation error it caches its downloads and draws such error, if you try to re-run.
Code:
C:\Users\user/.cache\torch\hub\checkpoints\resnet18-f37072fd.pth

title: How to Fix "RuntimeError: CUDA error: device-side assert triggered" in Pytorch
error_text: RuntimeError: CUDA error: device-side assert triggered
answer:
Root Cause: I also had this issue while training resnet model on google colab. So in my case I was training the model on 7 classes but the last layer of my network was set to output 3 classes.
Fix: Hence I changed  to this `self.classifier = torch.nn.Sequential(torch.nn.BatchNorm1d(512), torch.nn.Linear(512, 7)) ` After doing that also, i was still getting the error, because I didn't restart the google colab.
Code:
self.classifier = torch.nn.Sequential(torch.nn.BatchNorm1d(512), torch.nn.Linear(512, 3))

title: TransformerEncoder with a padding mask
error_text: RuntimeError: The size of tensor a (20) must match the size of tensor b (95) at non-singleton dimension 2
answer:
Root Cause: The required shapes are shown in `nn.Transformer.forward` - Shape (all building blocks of the transformer refer to it).
Fix: The padding mask should have shape [95, 20], not [20, 95]. This assumes that your batch size is 95 and the sequence length is 20, but if that is the other way around, you would have to transpose the `src` instead.
Code:
forward(src, mask=None, src_key_padding_mask=None)

title: How to mask a 3D tensor with 2D mask and keep the dimensions of original vector?
error_text: RuntimeError: The size of tensor a (2) must match the size of tensor b (3) at non-singleton dimension 2
answer:
Root Cause: Essentially, we need to match the dimension of the tensor mask with the tensor being masked. There are two ways to do it.
Fix: Approach 1: Does not preserve original tensor dimensions. The output for approach 1: `tensor([ 0, 1, 8, 9, 18, 19]) ` Approach 2: Preserves the original tensor dimensions (by padding).
Code:
X = torch.arange(24).view(4, 3, 2)
print(X)
mask = torch.zeros((4, 3), dtype=torch.int64)  # or dtype=torch.ByteTensor
mask[0, 0] = 1
mask[1, 1] = 1
mask[3, 0] = 1
print('Mask: ', mask)
# Add a dimension to the mask tensor and expand it to the size of original tensor

Fix: See the init function here.
Code:
list_layers = model.named_children()

title: 1D CNN on Pytorch: mat1 and mat2 shapes cannot be multiplied (10x3 and 10x2)
error_text: RuntimeError: mat1 and mat2 shapes cannot be multiplied (10x3 and 10x2)
answer:
Root Cause: The shape of the output of the line `x = self.layer2(x)` (which is also the input of the next line `x = self.fc1(x)`) is `torch.Size([1, 10, 3])`.
Fix: I don't know what it is you're trying to do, but assuming what you want to do is; label the entire `500` size sequence to one of two labels, the you do this. label `10` timesteps each to one of two labels, then you do this.
Code:
# replace self.fc1 = nn.Linear(10* 1 * 1, 2) with
self.fc1 = nn.Linear(10 * 3, 2)
# replace x = self.fc1(x) with
x = x.view(1, -1)
x = self.fc1(x)

title: Pytorch inference CUDA out of memory when multiprocessing
error_text: CUDA out of memory error. To mitigate this I added `torch.cuda.empty_cache()` call which made things better. However, there are still occasional out of memory errors. Probably due to bad allocation/release timing.
answer:
Root Cause: From my experience of parallel training and inference, it is almost impossible to squeeze the last bit of the GPU memory.
Fix: Using semaphore is the typical way to restrict the number of parallel processes and automatically start a new process when there is an open slot.
Code:
semaphore = mp.BoundedSemaphore(n_process)
with mp.Pool(n_process, initializer=pool_init, initargs=(semaphore,)) as pool:
    # here, each process can access the shared variable pool_semaphore
def pool_init(semaphore):
    global pool_semaphore
    pool_semaphore = semaphore

title: node2vec requires torch-cluster
error_text: ImportError: `Node2Vec` requires `torch-cluster`.
answer:
Root Cause: The Node2Vec model requires the torch-cluster library, part of PyTorch Geometric (PyG).
Fix: `pip install torch-scatter torch-sparse torch-cluster torch-spline-conv torch-geometric`
Code:
pip install torch-cluster

title: dimension out of range (expected to be in range of [-2, 1], but got 2)
error_text: RuntimeError: dimension out of range (expected to be in range of [-2, 1], but got 2)
answer:
Root Cause: torch.mm: Performs a matrix multiplication of the matrices mat1 and mat2. If mat1 is a (n×m) tensor, mat2 is a (m×p) tensor, out will be a (n×p) tensor.
Fix: torch.bmm: Performs a batch matrix-matrix product of matrices stored in batch1 and batch2. batch1 and batch2 must be 3-D tensors each containing the same number of matrices.
Code:
import torch
x = torch.mm(torch.randn(100, 78) , torch.randn(78, 10))
bsize = 16
x = torch.bmm(torch.randn(bsize, 100, 78) , torch.randn(bsize, 78, 10))

error_text: RuntimeError: cuda runtime error (710) : device-side assert triggered

Root Cause: What was your loss function? I got this error too.
Fix: My problem was a `` classification and I was using a `crossEntropy` loss. As it say in the documentations, labels should be in the range `[0, C-1]` where `C` is number of classes.

multi-class

title: Pytorch - why does preallocating memory cause "trying to backward through the graph a second time"
error_text: RuntimeError: Trying to backward through the graph a second time, but
answer:
Root Cause: The error implies that the program is trying to backpropagate through a set of operations a second time.
Fix: Therefore, the second time you try to backpropagate it fails as the graph has already been deleted. Here's a detailed explanation of the same. Short answer Use `loss.backward(retain_graph=True)`.
Code:
Every loop's graph
out = self(x) -> loss = self.lossfn(out, y)

title: Getting CUDA out of memory

answer:
Root Cause: Fairly general question. Here is how I would think on this problem.
Fix: Try to set batch size (number of batches) to 1. If this fixed the problem you may try to find optimal batch size. If even for `` you get "RuntimeError: cuda runtime error (2) : out of memory" : Do not use linear layers that are too large.
Code:
bs=1

Fix: embedded = embedded.swapdims(0,2) conved = [F.relu(conv(embedded)).squeeze(3) for conv in self.convs] ... `

                            nn.Conv2d(in_channels = 1,
                                      out_channels = n_filters,
                                      kernel_size = (fs, embedding_dim))
                            for fs in filter_sizes

Root Cause: I will first tell you the reason for the fluctuations and then a possible way to solve it. REASON When you train a network, you calculate a gradient that would reduce the loss.
Fix: In order to do that, you need to backpropagate the loss.

title: ImportError: cannot import name 'mobilenet_v2' from 'torchvision.models'
error_text: ImportError: cannot import name 'mobilenet_v2' from 'torchvision.models' (C:\file_path\__init__.py)`

title: RuntimeError: CUDA out of memory. Tried to allocate 2.86 GiB (GPU 0; 10.92 GiB total capacity;...9.06 GiB reserved in total by PyTorch)
error_text: CUDA out of memory. Tried to allocate 2.86 GiB (GPU 0; 10.92 GiB total capacity; 9.02 GiB already allocated; 1.29 GiB free; 9.06 GiB reserved in total by PyTorch)
answer:
Root Cause: The main issue here is likely that `os.environ['CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES'] = '2'` is being set too late, after PyTorch has already initialized CUDA.
Fix: You need to set it before importing torch, or better yet, set it in your shell before running the script. Also, looking at your `nvidia-smi`, GPU 0 has 8601MiB used and 10.92 GiB capacity, which matches the error message exactly.
Code:
import os
os.environ['CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES'] = '2'
import torch

title: RuntimeError: CUDA out of memory. Tried to allocate
error_text: CUDA out of memory. Tried to allocate 20.00 MiB (GPU 0; 2.00 GiB total capacity; 1.23 GiB already allocated; 18.83 MiB free; 1.25 GiB reserved in total by PyTorch)
answer:
Root Cause: You can free up the existing memory by  note the process and kill using `sudo kill -9 ` PS: Please the process before killing it!.
Fix: DON'T KILL THE MODEL WHICH IS ONGOING TRAINING!
Code:
nvidia-smi

title: RuntimeError: Given groups=1, weight of size [64, 3, 3, 3], expected input[4, 5000, 5000, 3] to have 3 channels, but got 5000 channels instead
error_text: RuntimeError: Given groups=1, weight of size [64, 3, 3, 3], expected input[4, 5000, 5000, 3] to have 3 channels, but got 5000 channels instead
answer:
Root Cause: The order of dimensions in pytorch is different than what you expect.
Fix: That is, your dimensions are `batch`-`height`-`width`-`channel`. However, pytorch expects tensor dimensions to be in a different order: `batch`-`channel`-`height`-`width`.
Code:
model(inputs.permute(0, 3, 1, 2))

title: RuntimeError("grad can be implicitly created only for scalar outputs")
error_text: RuntimeError: grad can be implicitly created only for scalar outputs
answer:
Root Cause: The pytorch error you get means "you can only call backward on scalars, i.e 0-dimensional tensors". Here, according to your prints, `policy_loss`is not scalar, it's a 1x4 matrix.
Fix: As a consequence, so is `policy_loss + 0.5 * value_loss`. Thus your call to `backward` yields an error. You probably forgot to reduce your losses to a scalar (with functions like `norm` or `MSELoss` ...).
Code:
# loss is 1x4
loss = policy_loss + 0.5 * value_loss
# explicit gradient backprop with non-scalar tensor
loss.backward(torch.ones(1,4))

title: How do I install Pytorch 1.3.1 with CUDA enabled
error_text: ImportError: /lib64/libc.so.6: version `GLIBC_2.14' not found
answer:
Root Cause: Given that your system is running Ubuntu 16.04, it comes with glibc installed. You can check your version by typing ``.
Fix: Keep in mind that PyTorch is compiled on CentOS which runs glibc version 2.17. Then check the CUDA version installed on your system `nvcc --version` Then install PyTorch as follows e.g.
Code:
ldd --version

title: CUDA out of memory error when reloading Pytorch model
error_text: CUDA out of memory error when reloading Pytorch model
answer:
Root Cause: The issue is that I was trying to load to a new GPU (`cuda:2`) but originally saved the model and optimizer from a different GPU (`cuda:0`).
Fix: Adding `map_location=device_id` to each `torch.load` call fixed the problem:
Code:
model.to(device_id)
model = model.load_state_dict(torch.load(model_file_path, map_location=device_id))
optimizer = optimizer.load_state_dict(torch.load(optimizer_file_path, map_location=device_id))

title: PyTorch: Is it possible to differentiate a matrix?
error_text: RuntimeError: grad can be implicitly created only for scalar outputs

title: Getting gradient of vectorized function in pytorch
error_text: RuntimeError: grad can be implicitly created only for scalar outputs"
answer:
Root Cause: Here you can find relevant discussion about your error.
Fix: If you pass `4` (or more) inputs, each needs a value with respect to which you calculate gradient. You can pass `torch.ones_like` explicitly to `backward` like this:
Code:
import torch
x = torch.tensor([4.0, 2.0, 1.5, 0.5], requires_grad=True)
out = torch.sin(x) * torch.cos(x) + x.pow(2)
# Pass tensor of ones, each for each item in x
out.backward(torch.ones_like(x))
print(x.grad)

title: RuntimeError: CUDA error: device-side assert triggered on loss function
error_text: RuntimeError: CUDA error: device-side assert triggered
answer:
Root Cause: There might be two reasons of the error: As the log says `input_val` is not between the range [0; 1]. So you should ensure that model outputs are in that range.
Fix: You can use `torch.clamp()` of pytorch. Before calculating the loss add the following line:  Maybe you are sure that model outputs are in the range [0; 1].
Code:
out = out.clamp(0, 1)

title: bool value of Tensor with more than one value is ambiguous
error_text: UserWarning: nn.functional.tanh is deprecated. Use torch.tanh instead.
answer:
Root Cause: The issue originates from calling `torch.nn.MSELoss(out, target)` which is the constructor for the `MSELoss` which accepts `size_average` and `reduce` as the first and second optional positional arguments.
Fix: `criterion = torch.nn.MSELoss() for epoch in range(1000): out = model(data) loss = criterion(out, target) loss.backward() `
Code:
loss = torch.nn.MSELoss(out, target)

title: Module not found in pycharm (Windows)
error_text: No module named 'torch'
answer:
Root Cause: Ok I solved this problem: First install anaconda and open the prompt then type `` and `pip3 install torchvision`.
Fix: Then you go to the Run settings and click `Run...` and then you go to `Edit Configurations` and then you select the `Project Default` interpreter and apply and you should be done! Thanks to the ppl who helped me =)
Code:
conda install pytorch -c pytorch

title: PyTorch - RuntimeError: Error(s) in loading state_dict for VGG:
error_text: RuntimeError: Error(s) in loading state_dict for VGG:
answer:
Root Cause: The problem is that what is being saved is not the same as what is expected to be loaded.
Fix: The load method doesn't have any logic to look inside the dict. This should work:
Code:
import torch, torchvision.models
model = torchvision.models.vgg16()
path = 'test.pth'
torch.save(model.state_dict(), path) # nothing else here
model.load_state_dict(torch.load(path))

title: ImportError: No module named 'torchvision.datasets.mnist'
error_text: No module named 'torchvision.datasets.mnist'
answer:
Root Cause: If you're using anaconda distribution, first install torchvision using:  If the package is not installed, then it will be installed. Else, it will throw the message # All requested packages already installed.
Fix: After this, try to import the `torchvision.datasets` as you mentioned.
Code:
$ conda install -c conda-forge torchvision
